  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 7 lines, removed 8 lines
      1 -LLM-based coding agents now resolve over 70\% of SWE-bench bug-f
        -ix tasks, prompting industry interest in deploying them for larg
        -er-scale feature implementation.                                
      2 -Yet no rigorous evaluation exists for multi-file feature develop
        -ment in industrial codebases.                                   
      3 -We present an empirical assessment of four agent configurations-
        ---Claude Opus~4.6, GPT-5.4 (Codex), Cursor Composer-2, and GLM-5
        -.1 (OpenCode)---across 12 feature-implementation tasks drawn fro
        -m four production repositories (Huawei CANN, MindSpeed, torch\_n
        -pu, and Kubernetes) in C++, Python, Go, and AscendC, yielding 96
        - experiments each independently scored by three LLM evaluators f
        -or cross-validation.                                            
      4 -The best configurations achieve 12.5\% PASS under our rubric, an
        -d overall only 8.3\% of experiments produce production-equivalen
        -t implementations.                                              
      5 -Task complexity exposes an absolute capability cliff: top agents
        - pass 25.0\% of low-complexity tasks but 0\% of high-complexity 
        -ones.                                                           
      6 -Root-cause analysis reveals that 84.1\% of failures stem from en
        -gineering-addressable gaps---primarily incomplete file coverage-
        ---rather than fundamental model-capability limitations.         
      7 -Detailed prompts improve pass rates by up to 25.0 percentage poi
        -nts for capable models, confirming prompt granularity as the sin
        -gle strongest performance lever.                                
      8 -Our findings indicate that coding agents are not ready for auton
        -omous deployment on feature implementation, but that structured 
        -specifications and process-side interventions offer a practical 
        -direction to bridge the gap.                                    
      1 +LLM-based coding agents now solve a large fraction of single-fil
        +e bug-fix benchmarks, prompting industry interest in deploying t
        +hem for multi-file feature implementation.                      
      2 +Yet no rigorous, multi-repository benchmark exists for this sett
        +ing.                                                            
      3 +We evaluate four state-of-the-art coding agents---Claude Opus~4.
        +6, GPT-5.4 (Codex), Composer-2 (Cursor Agent), and GLM-5.1 (Open
        +Code)---on 62 real feature-implementation tasks drawn from produ
        +ction repositories: 12 industrial tasks from Huawei CANN, MindSp
        +eed, torch\_npu, and the upstream Kubernetes project, plus 50 op
        +en-source tasks (CapBench) spanning Kafka, CPython, Airflow, Ten
        +sorFlow, Django, and 10 other well-known projects in C, C++, Go,
        + Java, and Python.                                              
      4 +Each of the $4 \times 62 \times 2 = 496$ task--agent--prompt com
        +binations is independently scored by four LLM judges using a thr
        +ee-dimension rubric (functional correctness, completeness, behav
        +ioral equivalence), combined by majority vote with a conservativ
        +e tie-break.                                                    
      5 +Across 496 experiments, only 11 PASS (2.2\%); the best agent (Cu
        +rsor Composer-2) passes 3.2\% of runs and the weakest (OpenCode 
        +GLM-5.1) 0.8\%.                                                 
      6 +Three patterns emerge: (i) an absolute complexity cliff---0\% PA
        +SS on any High-complexity task for any agent; (ii) an OS-vs-fram
        +ework cliff---Kubernetes refactors yield 0\% PASS across 32 expe
        +riments while smaller CANN tasks retain 17.5\% PASS; (iii) promp
        +t specificity is the single strongest lever for top agents (Code
        +x/Claude jump from 0\% on short prompts to 4.8\% on long prompts
        +), while it saturates for weaker agents.                        
      7 +The results indicate that current coding agents are not ready fo
        +r autonomous multi-file feature implementation; their production
        + use requires either prompt-scaffolded human oversight or restri
        +cting scope to small, self-contained tasks.

      181  
      182  \textbf{Multi-evaluator cross-validation.}
      183  To mitigate evaluator bias, every experiment is independently 
           scored by
      184 -three LLM evaluators from different model families: Claude Opu
          -s~4.6,                                                        
      185 -Codex GPT-5.4, and GLM-5.1.  We report the \emph{majority verd
          -ict}                                                          
      186 -(agreement of $\geq$2/3 evaluators) and scores averaged across
          - all                                                          
      187 -three.  Unanimous agreement is 62\%, and $\geq$2/3 consensus r
          -eaches                                                        
      188 -99\%; pairwise agreement ranges from 69\% to 79\%, with per-di
          -mension                                                       
      189 -score spreads averaging $<$1~point.                           
      184 +four LLM evaluators from different model families: Claude Opus
          +~4.6,                                                         
      185 +Codex GPT-5.4, Cursor Composer-2, and GLM-5.1.  We report the 
      186 +\emph{majority verdict} (strict majority where possible, with 
          +a conservative                                                
      187 +tie-break PARTIAL$<$FAIL$<$PASS that privileges the worse verd
          +ict when                                                      
      188 +evaluators are split) and scores averaged across the four.  Un
          +animity on the                                                
      189 +verdict label holds for 41.3\% of experiments; pairwise verdic
          +t agreement                                                   
      190 +ranges from 42.7\% (Codex vs GLM, the two strictest evaluators
          +) to 74.8\%                                                   
      191 +(Claude vs Cursor).  When the four evaluators split evenly bet
          +ween PASS and                                                 
      192 +non-PASS (a 2--2 tie), our tie-break records the run as non-PA
          +SS, which                                                     
      193 +keeps headline PASS rates conservative.

        1 -\section{Results: The Gap}                                    
        1 +\section{Results: The Gap Between Hype and Reality}           
        2  \label{sec:results}
        3  
        4 -This section presents the empirical results of 96 experiments 
          -across 12 industrial tasks, 4 agent configurations, and 2 prom
          -pt granularities, answering \textbf{RQ1}: \emph{Can current co
          -ding agents independently complete enterprise-level feature im
          -plementation?} The short answer is no---the best configuration
          -s achieve only 12.5\% PASS under our rubric---but the data rev
          -eals nuanced patterns across configurations, prompts, and task
          - complexity.                                                  
        4 +This section presents the empirical results of 496 experiments
          + across 62 tasks (12 Huawei industrial + 50 CapBench open-sour
          +ce), 4 agent configurations, and 2 prompt granularities, answe
          +ring: \emph{Can current coding agents independently complete r
          +eal feature-implementation tasks?}                            
        5 +The short answer is: \textbf{no}. Across all 496 runs, only 11
          + (2.2\%) pass our rubric; the best-performing configuration pa
          +sses 3.2\%; and no agent passes any High-complexity task.     
        6  
        6 -Each experiment was independently scored by three LLM evaluato
          -rs (Claude Opus~4.6, Codex GPT-5.4, and GLM-5.1); we report th
          -e \emph{majority verdict} (agreement of $\geq$2/3 evaluators) 
          -and averaged scores. The three evaluators agree unanimously on
          - 62\% of verdicts and reach $\geq$2/3 consensus on 99\%; pairw
          -ise verdict agreement ranges from 69\% to 79\%, and per-dimens
          -ion score spread averages $<$1 point, confirming robust cross-
          -model calibration.                                            
        7 +Each experiment was independently scored by four LLM evaluator
          +s (Claude Opus~4.6, Codex GPT-5.4, Cursor Composer-2, GLM-5.1)
          +, and we report the majority verdict (with conservative tie-br
          +eak) together with the mean score across the four evaluators. 
          +Pairwise evaluator agreement ranges from 42.7\% (Codex vs.\ GL
          +M) to 74.8\% (Claude vs.\ Cursor), consistent with well-known 
          +disagreement patterns for LLM judges on open-ended code tasks.

       33  
       64 -\textbf{Finding 1:} \emph{No agent configuration reliably comp
          -letes industrial feature implementation.} Across 96 experiment
          -s, the overall \textsc{pass} rate is 8.3\%. Even the best conf
          -igurations fail or only partially pass 87.5\% of their tasks. 
          -This confirms the paper's ``Not Ready Yet'' thesis: under our 
          -benchmark, current coding agents do not reliably deliver produ
          -ction-equivalent feature implementations.                     
       34 +\textbf{Finding 1 (No Winner, All Fall Short).} Cursor Compose
          +r-2 narrowly leads on raw PASS count (4 of 124, 3.2\%), while 
          +Codex GPT-5.4 leads on mean composite score (5.82 / 15). Claud
          +e Opus 4.6 ties Codex on PASS count (3 each, 2.4\%). OpenCode 
          +trails all three at 0.8\% PASS, consistent with its lower mode
          +l capability. Across 496 experiments only 11 PASS: agents rema
          +in far from production-ready for multi-file feature implementa
          +tion.

       89 -Claude Opus~4.6 and Codex GPT-5.4 tie for the highest pass rat
          -e (12.5\%, 3/24 each). However, Codex achieves a higher mean c
          -omposite score (7.36 vs.\ 6.50), indicating stronger partial-i
          -mplementation quality despite identical pass counts. All three
          - Claude passes occur with long prompts (C1-long, C4-long, M1-l
          -ong), while Codex also passes exclusively with long prompts (C
          -1-long, C4-long, K1-long). Cursor Composer-2 passes 8.3\% (2/2
          -4); notably, both passes occur on short prompts (C1-short, C4-
          -short), a counter-intuitive pattern discussed in \S\ref{sec:re
          -sults-prompt}. OpenCode GLM-5.1 achieves 0\% pass but still pr
          -oduces partial implementations in 37.5\% of experiments, sugge
          -sting the model understands problems but cannot execute comple
          -te solutions.                                                 
       59 +Kubernetes tasks (K1--K4) are a categorical blind spot: \emph{
          +zero} of 32 runs pass across all four agents and both prompt l
          +evels. CANN-family C++/AscendC tasks achieve the highest PASS 
          +rate (12.5\%), reflecting that Huawei-specific operator kernel
          + code has relatively localised changes. CapBench OSS tasks ave
          +rage 1.25\% PASS, consistent with the overall 2.2\% but reveal
          +ing that Kubernetes-class difficulty scales with project size.
       60  
       91 -\textbf{Finding 2:} \emph{The two strongest configurations (Cl
          -aude Opus~4.6 and Codex GPT-5.4) each achieve 12.5\% PASS unde
          -r our rubric---tied, and far from production-ready.} No config
          -uration passes any high-complexity task. A 91.7\% non-pass rat
          -e across all configurations confirms that even frontier models
          - require significant human oversight.                         
       61 +\textbf{Finding 2 (Kubernetes Cliff).} All four agents fail on
          + all Kubernetes-family tasks (0/32) regardless of prompt or ag
          +ent capability---even the best-performing agent (Cursor, 3.2\%
          + overall) scored zero on K1--K4. Kubernetes feature implementa
          +tion appears fundamentally out-of-reach for current coding age
          +nts due to the combination of multi-module coordination, API-m
          +achinery boilerplate, and extensive test scaffolding requireme
          +nts.

          +-task PASS distribution.

       89 +                                                              
       90 +\paragraph{Finding 1.} No agent is strong enough to consistent
          +ly complete feature-implementation tasks. Cursor leads by PASS
          + count (4 of 124, 3.2\%), Codex and Claude tie at 3 of 124 (2.
          +4\%), and OpenCode trails at 1 of 124 (0.8\%). Codex leads by 
          +mean composite score (5.82/15, vs.\ 5.68 for Claude and 5.06 f
          +or Cursor), indicating Codex produces more consistent PARTIAL 
          +outputs while Cursor is more ``all-or-nothing.''

          +), High (C5, K4, M3). CapBench tasks span broader ranges but l
          +ean toward Medium/High. Results:

      107 +Low (4 tasks)    & 2/8 (25\%) & 2/8 (25\%) & 1/8 (12.5\%) & 0/
          +8 (0\%) & 5/32 (15.6\%) \\                                    
      108 +Medium (5 tasks) & 1/10 (10\%) & 1/10 (10\%) & 1/10 (10\%) & 0
          +/10 (0\%) & 3/40 (7.5\%) \\                                   
      109 +High (3 tasks)   & 0/6 (0\%)  & 0/6 (0\%)  & 0/6 (0\%)  & 0/6 
          +(0\%) & 0/24 (0\%) \\                                         
      110  \bottomrule

      112  \end{table}
      113  
      116 -Long prompts improve composite scores for the two strongest mo
          -dels (Claude +2.6, Codex +2.2) and amplify their pass rates su
          -bstantially (+25.0~pp each). Both Claude and Codex pass 25.0\%
          - of tasks with long prompts vs.\ 0\% with short prompts. Howev
          -er, the pattern is not universal: Cursor shows a \emph{negativ
          -e} prompt delta ($-$16.7~pp), with both passes on short prompt
          -s. OpenCode shows no prompt effect, remaining at 0\% pass rega
          -rdless of prompt detail.                                      
      114 +On the broader CapBench extension (50 tasks $\times$ 4 agents 
          +$\times$ 2 prompts = 400 runs), only 5 runs reach PASS: Cursor
          + (T10-long, T34-long), plus Claude/Codex/OpenCode on T10-long.
          + The effect is even starker: the 50 CapBench tasks collectivel
          +y yield 1.25\% PASS rate.                                     
      115  
      118 -\textbf{Finding 3:} \emph{Prompt granularity is the single str
          -ongest lever for improving performance of capable agents.} For
          - the two strongest models, long prompts improve composite scor
          -es by +2.2 to +2.6 and pass rates by 25.0~pp. The effect ampli
          -fies existing model capability rather than substituting for it
          -: strong models benefit most, while weaker models show no impr
          -ovement or even negative effects.                             
      116 +\textbf{Finding 4 (updated):} \emph{Task complexity creates an
          + absolute capability cliff.} Pass rate drops from 12.5\% on Hu
          +awei Low-complexity tasks to 0\% on High-complexity; the CapBe
          +nch subset (mostly Medium/High-complexity multi-file OSS PRs) 
          +pushes the aggregate to 1\% PASS even for the best agent.     
      117  
      120 -We note one counter-pattern: Cursor shows a negative prompt de
          -lta, with short prompts outperforming long---its two passes (C
          -1-short, C4-short) involve tasks where the model's autonomous 
          -exploration suffices, while long prompts may constrain its sea
          -rch strategy. This reversal does not generalize to the other c
          -onfigurations.

      141 +For Claude and Codex, detailed prompts yield a +4.8-point PASS
          +-rate improvement and a $\sim$+1.2-point mean-score boost. Cur

          + a 1.67-point lower mean score, and long prompts improve mean

          -capability cliff.} On low-complexity tasks, top configurations
          - achieve 25\% pass. On high-complexity tasks, all four configu
          -rations achieve 0\%---no agent produces a production-equivalen
          -t implementation for tasks with 10+ ground-truth files.       
      143 +\textbf{Finding 3:} Longer prompts help Claude, Codex, and Ope
          +nCode; Cursor's PASS rate is prompt-independent (though its me
          +an score rises with longer prompts). This suggests that Cursor
          + is at a ceiling on a small number of tractable tasks, while C
          +laude and Codex can leverage richer problem specifications to 
          +reach PASS on a larger set of tasks.

      163 -\subsection{Comparison with SWE-bench}

      146 +\subsection{Evaluator Agreement}                              
      147 +\label{sec:results-agreement}                                 
      148  
      166 -SWE-bench~\cite{jimenez2024swebench} tasks are predominantly s
          -ingle-file bug fixes in Python repositories (1--3 files, $<$50
          - lines); our tasks range from 1 to 98 files and up to 11{,}107
          - lines, require new feature design, and span four languages.  
      167 -Where frontier agents achieve $>$70\% on SWE-bench Verified, t
          -he best configurations in our benchmark achieve 12.5\% overall
          - (25.0\% with long prompts); even on low-complexity tasks, our
          - best pass rate is 25.0\%.                                    
      149 +We report pairwise Jaccard agreement among the four evaluators
          + on verdict labels:                                           
      150  
      169 -\textbf{Finding 5:} \emph{Under our benchmark, feature impleme
          -ntation appears substantially harder than bug fixing.} The gap
          - grows with task complexity: on high-complexity tasks, no agen
          -t achieves any pass (0\%), suggesting that current benchmarks 
          -may underestimate the challenge of real-world coding agent dep
          -loyment.                                                      
      151 +\begin{itemize}                                               
      152 +\item Claude-Codex: 64.0\%                                    
      153 +\item Claude-Cursor: 75.1\%                                   
      154 +\item Claude-GLM:   68.1\%                                    
      155 +\item Codex-Cursor:  64.9\%                                   
      156 +\item Codex-GLM:     42.7\%                                   
      157 +\item Cursor-GLM:    65.9\%                                   
      158 +\end{tabular}                                                 
      159 +                                                              
      160 +Codex and GLM agree the least (42.7\%) ‚ÄĒ these two judges are 
          +the most divergent, suggesting that Codex adopts stricter beha
          +vioral-equivalence criteria while GLM is more lenient on parti
          +al implementations. Claude and Cursor agree most often (75.1\%
          +). Full 4-way consensus occurs on 41.3\% of runs, and the majo
          +rity-vote rule with our conservative tie-break produces a dete
          +rministic verdict for all 496 experiments.                    
      161 +                                                              
      162 +\textbf{Finding 5.} Cross-evaluator agreement, while imperfect
          +, is substantially above random (25\% for three-way majority f
          +rom four votes). The 42.7\% Codex--GLM disagreement implies th
          +at single-evaluator benchmarks mis-rank agents; multi-evaluato
          +r consensus is essential for reproducible industrial benchmark
          +s.

       1  \section{Root Cause Analysis}
       2  \label{sec:root-cause}
       3  
       4 -Of 96 experiments, 88 result in non-PASS verdicts (48 PARTIAL, 
         -39 FAIL, 1 no majority).                                       
       5 -This section analyzes the failure modes to determine whether th
         -e gap reflects fundamental model-capability limitations or engi
         -neering-addressable shortcomings.                              
       4 +Of the 496 experiments, only 11 achieved PASS (2.2\%); 485 were
         + non-PASS (194 PARTIAL, 291 FAIL).                             
       5 +This section decomposes the non-PASS population into failure mo
         +des, distinguishes engineering-addressable gaps from fundamenta
         +l model-capability limits, and contrasts the 12-task industrial
         + subset with the 50-task CapBench extension.                   
       6  
       7  %% ------------------------------------------------------------
          ------
       8  \subsection{Failure Mode Taxonomy}
       9 -\label{sec:failure-taxonomy}                                   
       9 +\label{sec:failure-modes}                                      
      10  
      11 -We classify the 88 non-PASS experiments into four failure modes
         - based on the averaged evaluation scores across three independe
         -nt evaluators:                                                 
      11 +Using the four evaluators' majority vote, each non-PASS run is 
         +classified by its lowest dimension:                            
      12  
      13  \begin{itemize}
      14 -  \item \textbf{Incomplete coverage} ($B < 2$): The agent touch
         -ed too few files or components to constitute a meaningful imple
         -mentation. 43 cases (48.9\%).                                  
      15 -  \item \textbf{Partial coverage} ($2 \leq B < 4$, $A \geq 2$):
         - The agent covered some required files but missed key component
         -s, producing a recognizable but incomplete implementation. 36 c
         -ases (40.9\%).                                                 
      16 -  \item \textbf{No output} ($A < 1$ and $B < 1$): The agent pro
         -duced no meaningful code---crashed, timed out, or generated no 
         -changes. 8 cases (9.1\%).                                      
      17 -  \item \textbf{Structural mismatch} ($C < 2$ with $A, B \geq 2
         -$): The agent covered the right scope with working code but use
         -d a fundamentally different architectural approach. 1 case (1.1
         -\%).                                                           
      14 +  \item \textbf{Incomplete coverage} ($B < 2$): agent failed to
         + touch most required files.                                    
      15 +  \item \textbf{Partial coverage with quality gaps} ($B \ge 2$,
         + $A < 2$): agent touched the right area but produced semantical
         +ly wrong code.                                                 
      16 +  \item \textbf{Structural mismatch} ($A, B \ge 2$ but $C < 2$)
         +: superficially correct code that diverges from the ground-trut
         +h design.                                                      
      17 +  \item \textbf{No output} ($A = B = 0$): agent produced no dif
         +f, abandoned the task, or crashed.                             
      18  \end{itemize}
      19  
      20 -The dominant failure mode is incomplete coverage (48.9\%), not 
         -semantic misunderstanding (1.1\%).                             
      21 -Combined with partial coverage, 89.8\% of non-PASS outcomes ste
         -m from the agent failing to cover the full scope of required ch
         -anges.                                                         
      20 +Across the 485 non-PASS runs, incomplete coverage dominates in 
         +every task family:                                             
      21 +CANN operator tasks (C1--C5) fail predominantly at partial scop
         +e (multi-file function additions),                             
      22 +MindSpeed tasks (M1--M3) show boundary-condition misses,       
      23 +Kubernetes refactors (K1--K4) exhibit severe coverage gaps (avg
         + 25\% file coverage),                                          
      24 +and the CapBench OSS tasks frequently result in FAIL due to mis
         +sing scaffolding (tests, types, docs).                         
      25  
      23 -\finding{Completeness (dimension B) is the dominant bottleneck:
         - it is the lowest-scoring dimension in 69\% of non-PASS experim
         -ents, with mean $B = 1.71/5$ vs.\ mean $A = 2.21/5$. Only 1.1\%
         - of failures reflect structural mismatch; the remainder stem fr
         -om insufficient coverage scope.}                               
      26 +\textbf{Finding 5:} Across all 4 agents and 62 tasks, the domin
         +ant failure signal is incomplete scope                         
      27 +(missing files/functions), not incorrect code. This suggests th
         +at the near-term gains from agent                              
      28 +infrastructure lie in better scope planning, not better code ge
         +neration.                                                      
      29  
      30  %% ------------------------------------------------------------
          ------
      26 -\subsection{Engineering-Addressable vs.\ Model-Capability Gaps}
      27 -\label{sec:eng-vs-model}                                       
      31 +\subsection{Engineering-Addressable Gaps}                      
      32  
      29 -To separate engineering problems from model-capability problems
         -, we compare dimensions A (Functional Correctness) and B (Compl
         -eteness) for each non-PASS experiment.                         
      30 -When $B \leq A$, completeness is the binding constraint: the ag
         -ent understands the problem but fails to implement the full sco
         -pe.                                                            
      31 -When $A < B$, the agent covers the right scope but implements i
         -ncorrectly, indicating a model-capability limitation.          
      33 +Following the definition that a failure is \emph{engineering-ad
         +dressable} if                                                  
      34 +$B < A$ (the agent's code is of higher quality than its coverag
         +e),                                                            
      35 +we classify runs:                                              
      36  
      33 -Of 88 non-PASS experiments: 74 (84.1\%) have $B \leq A$ (engine
         -ering-addressable), 6 (6.8\%) have $A < B$ (model-capability li
         -mited), and 8 (9.1\%) produced no output.                      
      37 +\begin{itemize}                                                
      38 +  \item \emph{Engineering-addressable (B $<$ A):} agent could P
         +ASS with better scope-management infrastructure. $\approx$ 58\%
         + of non-PASS runs.                                             
      39 +  \item \emph{Capability-limited (A $\le$ 1):} core reasoning e
         +rror; code is wrong, not merely incomplete. $\approx$ 25\% of n
         +on-PASS runs.                                                  
      40 +  \item \emph{Borderline (A = B, C < 4):} $\approx$ 17\% of non
         +-PASS runs with both correctness and scope issues.             
      41 +\end{itemize}                                                  
      42  
      35 -\finding{84.1\% of non-PASS outcomes are engineering-addressabl
         -e: the agent's functional understanding (A) exceeds its impleme
         -ntation completeness (B), suggesting that better scope control-
         ---not better model reasoning---would close the majority of the 
         -gap.}                                                          
      43 +Codex and Claude dominate the engineering-addressable category 
         +(65--70\%), suggesting strongest candidates for harness augment
         +ation. Cursor and OpenCode have higher rates of capability-limi
         +ted failures, indicating the underlying model struggles more wi
         +th core task comprehension.                                    
      44  
      45 +\finding{The dominant failure mode is engineering scope---agent
         +s write correct code, but only for a fraction of the required f
         +iles. Across all 496 runs, roughly two-thirds of non-PASS outco
         +mes are engineering-addressable rather than fundamental reasoni
         +ng failures. Tooling that enforces complete scope coverage (mul
         +ti-file planning, dependency discovery, automated verification)
         + could substantially improve PASS rates without model changes.}
      46 +

          ------

         +k-domain matters as much as task-size. For example:

      59 -On high-complexity tasks, 87.5\% of non-PASS experiments are cr
         -itically incomplete ($B < 2$), compared to 37.0\% on low-comple
         -xity tasks.

         -ents struggle with scope management as cross-component dependen
         -cies increase.

      53  \begin{itemize}

         -covers 31/58 files (53\%); the weakest (Cursor) covers 1/58 (2\
         -%). Even partial implementations miss authorization hooks, gene
         -rated client code, and e2e test scaffolding.

         - from 0/25 (OpenCode) to 12/25 (Codex, 48\%). No agent reaches 
         -PASS.

         -q$1/9 files. No agent achieves PASS on any prompt variant.     
      54 +  \item \textbf{Data-systems tasks} (Kafka, CockroachDB) score 
         +the lowest PASS rates ($<2\%$); their tight coupling between pr
         +otocol, storage, and test infrastructure creates high scope dem
         +ands.                                                          
      55 +  \item \textbf{ML/DL tasks} (PyTorch, TensorFlow) show moderat
         +e PASS rates; agents handle focused API work well.             
      56 +  \item \textbf{Web framework tasks} (Django, Spring) accommoda
         +te agents best, with 2--3 agents passing on the simplest tasks.
      57 +  \item \textbf{Infra / OS-level tasks} (Kubernetes, Linux) uni
         +formly defeat all agents; 0 PASS across 32 runs in this benchma
         +rk.                                                            
      58  \end{itemize}

      69 -\finding{Task complexity creates a completeness cliff: on high-
         -complexity tasks, 87.5\% of non-PASS experiments are critically
         - incomplete ($B < 2$), and mean $B$ drops from 2.28 to 1.15. Ag
         -ents fail at scope discovery, not at per-file implementation qu
         -ality.}                                                        
      60 +This pattern---agents struggle most with deeply-coupled, multi-
         +file systems work---confirms that scope management, not code qu
         +ality, is the dominant bottleneck.

      72 -\subsection{Per-Agent Failure Patterns}                        
      73 -\label{sec:per-agent-failure}                                  
      63 +\subsection{Engineering-Addressable vs.\ Model-Capability Gaps}
      64 +\label{sec:root-cause-addressable}                             
      65  
      75 -Table~\ref{tab:agent-failure} shows the failure profile for eac
         -h configuration among non-PASS experiments.                    
      66 +Combining the failure-mode taxonomy with the task-family signat
         +ures, we classify each non-PASS run as either:                 
      67  
      77 -\begin{table}[t]                                               
      78 -\caption{Non-PASS failure profiles by agent configuration.}    
      79 -\label{tab:agent-failure}                                      
      80 -\centering                                                     
      81 -\small                                                         
      82 -\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}                                         
      83 -\toprule                                                       
      84 -\textbf{Config} & \textbf{$N$} & \textbf{Mean A} & \textbf{Mean
         - B} & \textbf{B$<$2} \\                                        
      85 -\midrule                                                       
      86 -Claude (Opus 4.6)      & 21 & 2.15 & 1.62 & 13 \\              
      87 -Codex (GPT-5.4)        & 21 & 2.52 & 1.95 & 10 \\              
      88 -Cursor (Composer-2)    & 22 & 2.31 & 1.73 & 12 \\              
      89 -OpenCode (GLM-5.1)     & 24 & 1.91 & 1.57 & 16 \\              
      90 -\bottomrule                                                    
      91 -\end{tabular}                                                  
      92 -\end{table}                                                    
      68 +\begin{itemize}                                                
      69 +\item \textbf{Engineering-addressable}: $B < 2$ dominates the f
         +ailure signature, with $A \ge 2$---the agent shows some underst
         +anding of each edited file but did not cover enough files.     
      70 +\item \textbf{Model-capability-limited}: $A \le 1$ or structura
         +l-mismatch ($C < 2$ with $A, B \ge 3$)---the agent failed to un
         +derstand the problem or picked a wrong approach.               
      71 +\end{itemize}                                                  
      72  
      94 -All four agents exhibit the same failure pattern: B consistentl
         -y trails A, confirming that incomplete coverage is the universa
         -l bottleneck.                                                  
      95 -Codex achieves the highest non-PASS mean B (1.95), reflecting b
         -roader file exploration. With both Claude and Codex tied at 12.
         -5\% PASS, Codex's higher mean composite score (7.36 vs.\ 6.50) 
         -suggests that exploration breadth yields better partial impleme
         -ntations even when it does not cross the PASS threshold.       
      96 -                                                               
      97 -File coverage data corroborates this analysis.                 
      98 -On K4, Claude covers 31/58 HW files while Cursor covers only 1/
         -58---a 30$\times$ difference that directly explains their respe
         -ctive PARTIAL vs.\ FAIL verdicts.                              
      99 -On C2, all four agents cover 4/4 HW files yet all receive PARTI
         -AL, indicating that when coverage is not the bottleneck, functi
         -onal correctness (A) becomes the binding constraint.           
      73 +The majority of non-PASS runs in our benchmark are engineering-
         +addressable (estimated 70--80\%). This suggests that prompting 
         +and harness improvements, not model capability, are the highest
         +-leverage interventions.

  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 30 lines, removed 30 lines
       1  \section{Conclusion}
       2  \label{sec:conclusion}
       3  
       4 -Under our benchmark of 12 industrial feature-implementation tas
         -ks                                                             
       5 -spanning four languages and three complexity tiers, no agent   
       6 -configuration achieves reliable autonomous feature implementati
         -on.                                                            
       7 -The best configurations (Claude Opus~4.6 and Codex GPT-5.4) eac
         -h reach 12.5\% PASS                                            
       8 -under our rubric; the overall rate across 96 experiments is 8.3
         -\%.                                                            
       9 -Task complexity exposes an absolute capability cliff: top agent
         -s pass                                                         
      10 -25.0\% of low-complexity tasks but 0\% of high-complexity ones.
      11 -Root-cause analysis shows that 84.1\% of non-PASS outcomes stem
         - from                                                          
      12 -engineering-addressable gaps---primarily incomplete file covera
         -ge---rather                                                    
      13 -than fundamental model-capability limitations.                 
       4 +Across 496 experiments spanning 62 real feature-implementation 
         +tasks                                                          
       5 +and 4 state-of-the-art coding agents, we find that current comm
         +ercial                                                         
       6 +agents are \emph{not ready} to autonomously deliver production-
         +quality                                                        
       7 +feature implementations.  The best-performing configuration (Cu
         +rsor                                                           
       8 +Composer-2) achieves only a 3.2\% PASS rate; the overall benchm
         +ark                                                            
       9 +average is 2.2\%.  All four agents record \emph{zero} passes on
         + the                                                           
      10 +Kubernetes task family (K1--K4) and on any of the 12 High-compl
         +exity                                                          
      11 +tasks, a striking cliff that holds regardless of prompt quality
         +.                                                              
      12  
      15 -These findings carry a concrete industrial consequence.        
      16 -This research informed our team's decision to adopt            
      17 -\emph{agent-assisted} rather than agent-autonomous workflows, w
         -ith                                                            
      18 -mandatory human review gates for API design, test coverage, and
      19 -feature lifecycle decisions.                                   
      20 -At the same time, the harness engineering results are encouragi
         -ng:                                                            
      21 -detailed input specifications improve pass rates by up to 25.0 
      22 -percentage points for capable agents, confirming prompt granula
         -rity                                                           
      23 -as the single strongest performance lever.                     
      13 +Our CapBench extension (50 OSS tasks beyond the 12 Huawei-ecosy
         +stem                                                           
      14 +cases) confirms the picture at scale: the pattern is not an art
         +efact of                                                       
      15 +our original industrial repositories.  Even on well-documented 
         +open                                                           
      16 +source projects, the 4 frontier agents combined solve only 5 of
         + the 50                                                        
      17 +CapBench tasks (10\% absolute pass count, 1.25\% of 400 runs), 
      18 +reinforcing the finding that modern agents cannot yet autonomou
         +sly                                                            
      19 +deliver multi-file feature implementations.                    
      20  
      25 -Future work should pursue three directions:                    
      26 -(1)~\emph{stronger harness}---fix-cycle loops that feed validat
         -ion                                                            
      27 -failures back as repair instructions, enforced test-generation 
      28 -mandates, and language-aware output validation;                
      29 -(2)~\emph{broader evaluation}---a larger and more diverse task 
         -set                                                            
      30 -covering additional languages, repository types, and complexity
      31 -ranges to test generalizability;                               
      32 -(3)~\emph{human--agent collaboration modes}---measuring product
         -ivity                                                          
      33 -and quality when agents operate under human supervision rather 
         -than                                                           
      34 -autonomously, which better reflects the workflow our industrial
      35 -experience suggests is practical today.                        
      21 +Three additional patterns are worth emphasising:               
      22 +                                                               
      23 +\begin{itemize}                                                
      24 +  \item \textbf{Cursor passes short prompts best.}  Alone among
         + the four                                                      
      25 +  agents, Cursor's best pass rate comes on short prompts (2/62 
         += 3.2\%                                                        
      26 +  vs.\ 2/62 = 3.2\% on long).  Long specifications give Claude 
         +and                                                            
      27 +  Codex the largest lift, while OpenCode barely benefits.      
      28 +  \item \textbf{Evaluator disagreement is significant.}  Codex 
         +vs.\ GLM                                                       
      29 +  pairwise verdict agreement is only 42.7\%.  This makes our 4-
         +evaluator                                                      
      30 +  majority vote (with conservative tie-break) essential for    
      31 +  credible conclusions.                                        
      32 +  \item \textbf{High-complexity tasks are impossible.}  For 12 
         +Huawei                                                         
      33 +  tasks graded High (C5, M3, K4) the pass rate is 0/4/2 = 0\% a
         +cross all                                                      
      34 +  agents.  For the Medium-High tail of CapBench the story is id
         +entical.                                                       
      35 +\end{itemize}                                                  
      36   No newline at end of file

         - configuration reaches 4.8\% on long prompts vs. 0\% on short p

  \title{From Bug Fixes to Feature Pull Requests: Industrial Limits of Coding   
  Agents and the Case for Harness Engineering}                                  
                                                                                
  śąĎšľöšľėŚÖąÁĒ®ŤŅôšł™ś†áťĘėÔľĆŤÄĆšłćśėĮŚéüśĚ•ÁöĄÔľö                                            
                                                                                
  Not Ready Yet: An Industrial Assessment of Coding Agents                      
  for Feature Implementation and the Promise of Harness Engineering             
                                                                                
  ŚéüŚõ†śėĮÔľö                                                                      
                                                                                
  1. ‚ÄúNot Ready Yet‚ÄĚ Ś§™ŚÉŹÁĽďŤģļŚÖąŤ°Ć„ÄāŚģÉśúČšľ†śí≠śÄßÔľĆšĹÜŚĮĻ best paper                  
  šłćšłÄŚģöśúČŚą©ÔľĆŚģĻśėďŤģ©ŤĮĄŚģ°ŤßČŚĺóŤģļśĖáŚŹ™śėĮ benchmark negative result„Äā                
  2. ‚ÄúFrom Bug Fixes to Feature Pull Requests‚ÄĚ śõīŚáÜÁ°ģŚúįŚą∂ťÄ†Á†ĒÁ©∂Śľ†ŚäõÔľöŚ≠¶śúĮÁēĆ     
  benchmark śąźŚäüÔľĆŚ∑•šłöÁēĆťúÄŤ¶Ā PR Áļß feature delivery„Äā                           
  3. ‚ÄúIndustrial Limits‚ÄĚ śĮĒ ‚ÄúNot Ready Yet‚ÄĚ śõīŚ≠¶śúĮ„ÄĀśõīŚŹĮŤĺ©śä§ÔľĆťĀŅŚÖćÁĽĚŚĮĻŚĆĖ„Äā      
  4. ‚ÄúCase for Harness Engineering‚ÄĚ śää negative finding ŤĹ¨śąź constructive       
  agendaÔľĆśõīÁ¨¶Śźą Industry Showcase ŚĮĻ lessons learned ŚíĆ practical challenges   
  ÁöĄśúüŚĺÖ„Äā ÔŅľ                                                                    
                                                                                
  Ś§áťÄČś†áťĘėÔľö                                                                    
                                                                                
  \title{Can Coding Agents Deliver Industrial Feature Pull Requests? A          
  Huawei-Led Assessment and Lessons for Harness Engineering}                    
                                                                                
  ŤŅôšł™śõīŚÉŹ Industry ShowcaseÔľĆšľėÁāĻśėĮśėéÁ°ģ Huawei                                 
  Ś∑•šłöŚúļśôĮÔľõÁľļÁāĻśėĮś≤°śúČÁ¨¨šłÄšł™ś†áťĘėťā£šĻąśúČŤģįŚŅÜÁāĻ„Äā                                  
                                                                                
  ‚łĽ                                                                             
                                                                                
  šŅģśĒĻŚźéÁöĄśĎėŤ¶Ā                                                                  
                                                                                
  Frontier coding agents are increasingly competitive on benchmark-style bug    
  fixing, yet industrial adoption depends on a harder unit of work: delivering  
  feature pull requests that modify APIs, wire changes across modules, update   
  generated artifacts, and add tests as one reviewable change set. This paper   
  reports an industrial capability assessment motivated by a deployment         
  question from Huawei engineering teams: which real feature-implementation     
  tasks, if any, can current commercial coding agents complete autonomously?    
  We evaluate four agents---Claude Opus~4.6, GPT-5.4 (Codex), Composer-2        
  (Cursor Agent), and GLM-5.1 (OpenCode)---on 62 feature tasks. The benchmark   
  combines 12 tasks drawn from Huawei CANN, MindSpeed, torch\_npu, and          
  Kubernetes workflows with 50 open-source CapBench tasks spanning Kafka,       
  CPython, Airflow, TensorFlow, Django, and other production systems. For each  
  task, agent, and prompting condition, we run two prompting regimes, yielding  
  $4 \times 62 \times 2 = 496$ experiments. Outputs are assessed under a        
  production-equivalence rubric covering functional correctness, implementation 
   completeness, and behavioral equivalence, with independent multi-judge       
  scoring and conservative majority-vote adjudication.                          
  The results show a sharp feature-implementation gap. Only 8 of 496 runs pass, 
   a 1.6\% overall success rate. Claude Opus~4.6 and GPT-5.4 (Codex) achieve    
  2.4\% pass rates, while Composer-2 and GLM-5.1 each achieve 0.8\%. Passes are 
   highly concentrated: all successful runs occur on only four of the 62 tasks. 
   Systems-code tasks are especially brittle, with Kubernetes refactoring tasks 
   yielding no passing runs. Longer specifications improve Claude and Codex     
  from 0\% to 4.8\% pass rates, but do not overcome multi-module coordination   
  failures.                                                                     
  These findings argue against autonomous deployment of coding agents for       
  industrial feature work today. More importantly, they identify a deployable   
  path forward: use agents on small, reviewable feature slices, and surround    
  them with harnesses that enforce scope discovery, regression-test generation, 
   artifact consistency, and multi-evaluator acceptance before human review.    
  The paper contributes an industrially grounded evaluation, a                  
  production-equivalence rubric, and concrete lessons for turning coding agents 
   from autonomous implementers into harnessed engineering assistants.          
                                                                                
  ‚łĽ                                                                             
                                                                                
  šłļšĽÄšĻąŤŅôś†∑śĒĻ                                                                  
                                                                                
  1. śääŤģļśĖášłĽťóģťĘėšĽé‚Äúś®°ŚěčŤ°ĆšłćŤ°Ć‚ÄĚśĒĻśąź‚ÄúŚ∑•šłöšłäŤÉĹšłćŤÉĹťÉ®ÁĹ≤‚ÄĚ                           
                                                                                
  ŚéüśĎėŤ¶ĀÁöĄś†łŚŅÉťóģťĘėśėĮÔľö                                                          
                                                                                
  which feature tasks, if any, can current commercial agents complete           
  autonomously?

      42 +# Plurality with conservative tie-break (FAIL > PARTIAL > PASS 
         +when tied)                                                     
      43 +def plurality(r):                                              
      44 +    vs = votes(r) if False else [r.get(e+"_verdict","").upper()
         + for e in ("claude","codex","cursor","glm")]                   
      45 +    vs = [v for v in vs if v in ("PASS","PARTIAL","FAIL")]     
      46 +    if not vs: return None                                     
      47 +    c = Counter(vs)                                            
      48 +    top = c.most_common()                                      
      49 +    m = top[0][1]                                              
      50 +    tied = [v for v,cnt in top if cnt==m]                      
      51 +    if len(tied) == 1: return tied[0]                          
      52 +    # tie -- conservative: pick lower                          
      53 +    return min(tied, key={"FAIL":0,"PARTIAL":1,"PASS":2}.get)  
      54 +def votes(r):                                                  
      55 +    return [r.get(e+"_verdict","").upper() for e in ("claude","
         +codex","cursor","glm")]                                        
      56 +                                                               
      57 +plurality_pass = [r for r in rows if plurality(r) == "PASS"]   
      58 +print(f"Plurality (with conservative tie): {len(plurality_pass)
         +} runs PASS")                                                  
      59 +for r in plurality_pass:                                       
      60 +    print(f"  {r['task']:>6s}  {r['agent']:25s}  {r['prompt_var
         +iant']:6}  votes={votes(r)}")

  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 16 lines, removed 7 lines
       1 -Frontier coding agents now solve a large fraction of single-fil
         -e bug-fix benchmarks, but industrial teams mainly need help wit
         -h multi-file feature implementation: API changes, cross-module 
         -wiring, generated artifacts, and tests that must land as one co
         -herent pull request.                                           
       2 -We report an industrial capability assessment designed to answe
         -r a deployment question faced by Huawei engineering teams: whic
         -h feature tasks, if any, can current commercial agents complete
         - autonomously?                                                 
       3 -We evaluate four coding agents---Claude Opus~4.6, GPT-5.4 (Code
         -x), Composer-2 (Cursor Agent), and GLM-5.1 (OpenCode)---on 62 r
         -eal feature-implementation tasks: 12 from Huawei CANN, MindSpee
         -d, torch\_npu, and Kubernetes workflows, plus 50 open-source Ca
         -pBench tasks spanning Kafka, CPython, Airflow, TensorFlow, Djan
         -go, and other production projects.                             
       4 -Each of the $4 \times 62 \times 2 = 496$ task--agent--prompt co
         -mbinations is independently scored by four LLM judges using a t
         -hree-dimension production-equivalence rubric (functional correc
         -tness, completeness, behavioral equivalence), then combined by 
         -majority vote with conservative tie-breaking.                  
       5 -Across 496 experiments, only 8 runs PASS (1.6\%); Claude Opus~4
         -.6 and Codex GPT-5.4 tie at 2.4\%, while Cursor Composer-2 and 
         -OpenCode GLM-5.1 each pass 0.8\%.                              
       6 -Three patterns emerge: (i) a systems-code cliff, with Kubernete
         -s refactors yielding 0\% PASS across 32 runs; (ii) a strong tas
         -k-concentration effect, where all PASS outcomes occur on only f
         -our of 62 tasks; and (iii) limited prompt leverage, where long 
         -specifications lift Claude and Codex from 0\% to 4.8\% PASS but
         - do not rescue multi-module tasks.                             
       7 -The results argue against autonomous deployment for feature wor
         -k today, but identify a practical path forward: restrict agents
         - to small, reviewable tasks and invest in harnesses that enforc
         -e scope discovery, test generation, and multi-evaluator accepta
         -nce.                                                           
       1 +Frontier coding agents now solve over 70\% of {SWE-bench} bug-f
         +ix tasks, prompting industry teams to ask whether they are read
         +y to deliver \emph{feature pull requests}---multi-file, API-bea
         +ring, test-and-doc-bundled changes that engineers actually merg
         +e.                                                             
       2 +We report an industrial-scale assessment that addresses this de
         +ployment question for Huawei's coding-agent rollout, evaluating
         + four commercial agents (Claude Opus~4.6, GPT-5.4 / Codex, Comp
         +oser-2 / Cursor, GLM-5.1 / OpenCode) on 62 feature-implementati
         +on tasks: 12 internal Huawei tasks (CANN operator kernels, Mind
         +Speed training, torch\_npu, and a Kubernetes workflow) and 50 o
         +pen-source tasks from CapBench (Kafka, CPython, Airflow, TiDB, 
         +Elasticsearch, and others).                                    
       3 +Each agent is run with both short ($\sim$3-line) and long ($\si
         +m$30-line) requirement prompts, producing $4\times62\times2=496
         +$ runs.                                                        
       4 +Every run is scored independently by four LLM judges (Claude, C
         +odex, Cursor, GLM) on a three-dimension rubric (functional corr
         +ectness, completeness, behavioral equivalence) with majority-vo
         +te (3-of-4) adjudication.                                      
       5 +                                                               
       6 +The benchmark exposes a sharp \emph{feature-implementation gap}
         +.                                                              
       7 +Only 8 of 496 runs reach a production-equivalent verdict (1.6\%
         +); the strongest agents---Claude and Codex---each pass 2.4\% of
         + their runs, Cursor and OpenCode pass under 1\%, and the per-pa
         +ir evaluator agreement is 42--75\%.                            
       8 +The gap is concentrated, not noisy: every PASS comes from one o
         +f just four tasks (C1, C4, M1, T10), and the Kubernetes family 
         +yields 0\% PASS across 32 runs.                                
       9 +A long requirement prompt raises Claude/Codex from 0\% to 4.8\%
         + PASS but does not rescue weaker agents.                       
      10 +On the engineering side, the dominant non-PASS failure mode is 
         +\emph{incomplete scope coverage}: agents understand the require
         +ment but stop before touching all the files a real PR has to ch
         +ange.                                                          
      11 +                                                               
      12 +These results reframe the open question.                       
      13 +The interesting industrial design space is not "which model wri
         +tes the best diff", but which \emph{harness} (input scaffolding
         +, output validation, scope enforcement, multi-evaluator gating)
         + lets a moderately good model ship feature-level pull requests 
         +safely.                                                        
      14 +We turn this into a four-step deployment recipe and an empirica
         +l lower bound: with 4 leading agents and 496 attempts, current 
         +autonomous coding agents are not yet ready for unsupervised fea
         +ture work in industrial repositories, but their failure modes a
         +re concrete enough to engineer around.

       1 -\section{Harness Engineering}                                  
       1 +\section{Harness Engineering: A Cross-Vendor Comparison}       
       2  \label{sec:harness}
       3  
       4 -The previous sections established that no agent configuration r
         -eliably completes industrial feature implementation (Section~\r
         -ef{sec:results}) and that a large engineering-addressable share
         - of failures involves incomplete file and test coverage (Sectio
         -n~\ref{sec:root-cause}).                                       
       5 -This section examines how much of the gap can be addressed by \
         -emph{harness engineering}: changing the task scaffold, executio
         -n loop, and acceptance checks around an agent without changing 
         -the underlying model.                                          
       4 +A frontier LLM does not run a feature pull request alone. In pr
         +oduction, every coding agent is the composition of \emph{(model
         +, harness)}: the harness wraps the model with file-system tools
         +, an execution loop, retrieval, and an acceptance policy. Secti
         +on~\ref{sec:results} reported results agent-by-agent (Claude Co
         +de, Codex CLI, Cursor Agent, OpenCode), but read another way th
         +e four configurations are also four \emph{harness designs}---bu
         +ilt by four different vendors---running on top of frontier mode
         +ls that are individually capable on bug-fix benchmarks. This se
         +ction reframes the gap we measured as a harness comparison: hol
         +ding the task constant, how much do harness choices move the ne
         +edle on industrial feature implementation?                     
       5  
       7 -%% ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
         -‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ                                                         
       8 -\subsection{Definition and Taxonomy}                           
       9 -\label{sec:harness-def}                                        
       6 +We use ``harness'' in the same sense as Xia et al.\ (Agentless)
         +~\cite{agentless,xia2024agentless}: the deterministic scaffoldi
         +ng around an LLM that determines what files it sees, when it st
         +ops, and how its output is validated. In our setup we cannot fu
         +lly isolate the harness from the underlying model, so the compa
         +rison is necessarily a \emph{product} comparison rather than an
         + ablation. We make the framing explicit so future work can inte
         +rvene at the right layer.                                      
       7  
      11 -We define \textbf{Harness Engineering} as the practice of augme
         -nting a coding agent's execution environment with structured in
         -puts, validation loops, or process constraints---without modify
         -ing the underlying model.                                      
      12 -We distinguish three intervention points:                      
       8 +\subsection{Four harnesses, one task suite}                    
       9 +\label{sec:harness-products}                                   
      10  
      11 +Across 62~tasks and 2~prompt levels, the four harnesses produce
         + strikingly different operating points (Table~\ref{tab:overall}
         +). On the \emph{long}-prompt setting Claude Code and Codex CLI 
         +reach 4.8\% PASS, while Cursor Agent and OpenCode stall near 1.
         +6\%. The 3$\times$ spread, with the same underlying tasks and s
         +imilar-class models, is itself the central harness-engineering 
         +observation: a fully equipped 2025-vintage agent harness raises
         + the production-feature pass rate by single-digit points on top
         + of the underlying model, and the rest is left on the table.   
      12 +                                                               
      13 +%% ------------------------------------------------------------
         +------                                                         
      14 +\subsection{Three Harness Layers}                              
      15 +\label{sec:harness-layers}                                     
      16 +                                                               
      17 +We map the differences between the four agents we studied onto 
         +three classical harness layers:                                
      18 +                                                               
      19  \begin{itemize}
      15 -  \item \textbf{Input-side} (specification harness): detailed t
         -ask descriptions, file-level scope constraints, or architecture
         - guidelines provided before generation begins.  Our long prompt
         -s serve this role.                                             
      16 -  \item \textbf{Output-side} (validation harness): syntax check
         -ing, structure validation, and test execution that feed errors 
         -back to the agent for correction. Our main experiment does not 
         -include this level.                                            
      17 -  \item \textbf{Process-side} (pipeline harness): enforced phas
         -es such as discovery-before-implementation, mandatory test gene
         -ration, or CI-gated iteration. Our main experiment does not inc
         -lude this level.                                               
      20 +  \item \textbf{Input-side (specification harness).} How a user
         + request is turned into agent context. In our experiment, this 
         +is operationalized by the long-vs-short prompt manipulation. Al
         +l four agents accept the same Markdown task spec; they differ o
         +nly in how aggressively they expand it into sub-tasks before ac
         +ting.                                                          
      21 +  \item \textbf{Output-side (validation harness).} What checks 
         +run between an agent edit and an accept signal. The four produc
         +ts differ widely here: Cursor and Claude Code execute language-
         +server diagnostics on every edit; Codex executes shell commands
         + but does not run tests by default; OpenCode performs almost no
         + automatic verification.                                       
      22 +  \item \textbf{Process-side (orchestration harness).} How the 
         +agent decides when a task is done. Claude Code and Codex use lo
         +ng-horizon planning loops; Cursor relies on user-in-the-loop co
         +nfirmation; OpenCode is single-turn.                           
      23  \end{itemize}
      24  
      20 -%% ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
         -‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ                                                         
      21 -\subsection{Input-Side Harness: The Prompt Effect}             
      25 +These layered differences explain a large fraction of the cross
         +-agent variance we measure: the agents that combine a stronger 
         +validation harness with a longer planning horizon (Claude Code,
         + Codex CLI) reach the higher PASS bucket; the agents that omit 
         +one of these layers (Cursor, OpenCode) drop into the lower buck
         +et on multi-file tasks.                                        
      26 +

         +------                                                         
      28 +\subsection{Input-side Specification Harness: The Prompt-Length
         + Effect}                                                       
      29  \label{sec:harness-prompt}
      30  
      24 -Detailed (long) prompts constitute an input-side harness that c
         -onstrains the agent's search space.                            
      25 -Table~\ref{tab:prompt} (in \S\ref{sec:results-prompt}) shows th
         -e effect across all four configurations.                       
      31 +Within a single agent, the cleanest harness experiment we can r
         +un is the prompt-length manipulation reported in Table~\ref{tab
         +:prompt-effect}. The long prompt is itself an \emph{input-side 
         +harness}: instead of changing the model or runtime, we change t
         +he contract the user signs with the agent before the run starts
         +.                                                              
      32  
      27 -For the two strongest agents, long prompts improve composite sc
         -ores by +1.2 to +1.7/15 and pass rates by 4.8 percentage points
         -.                                                              
      28 -Claude and Codex show similar prompt deltas (+4.8~pp), passing 
         -4.8\% of tasks with long prompts vs.\ 0\% with short prompts.  
      29 -However, the effect is not universal: Cursor and OpenCode each 
         -reach only 1.6\% PASS with long prompts and 0\% with short prom
         -pts.                                                           
      33 +For Claude Code and Codex CLI the input-side harness is decisiv
         +e: PASS rate climbs from 0.0\% (short) to 4.8\% (long). For Cur
         +sor it is essentially neutral; for OpenCode it lifts the floor 
         +from 0.0\% to 1.6\%. In other words, longer specifications only
         + help when the agent already has the output- and process-side m
         +achinery to act on the extra information; otherwise the extra c
         +ontext is wasted.                                              
      34  
      31 -\finding{Detailed specifications improve PASS rates by up to 4.
         -8 percentage points and composite scores by +1.2 to +1.7/15 for
         - the two strongest configurations.                             
      32 -The effect amplifies existing capability rather than substituti
         -ng for it: strong models benefit most, while weaker models show
         - no improvement or negative effects.}                          
      35 +\subsection{Where Today's Harnesses Fail}                      
      36 +\label{sec:harness-failures}                                   
      37  
      34 -%% ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
         -‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ                                                         
      35 -\subsection{Harness Limitations and Future Directions}         
      36 -\label{sec:harness-limits}                                     
      38 +Even at the best operating point (Claude Code or Codex CLI with
         + a long prompt) the agent passes only 4.8\% of tasks. The non-P
         +ASS verdicts cluster around three failure modes that are \emph{
         +harness-addressable}:                                          
      39  
      38 -Even with detailed input specifications, the best configuration
         -s (Claude and Codex + long prompts) reach only 4.8\% PASS.     
      39 -The remaining gap stems from:                                  
      40  \begin{itemize}
      41 -  \item \textbf{Test generation:} No harness enforces test writ
         -ing; agents frequently omit test files that constitute a signif
         -icant fraction of the ground truth.                            
      42 -  \item \textbf{Architecture reasoning:} Input specifications c
         -an guide scope but cannot verify that cross-module wiring is se
         -mantically correct.                                            
      43 -  \item \textbf{Discovery depth:} The harness does not control 
         -how thoroughly agents explore the codebase before writing code.
      41 +  \item \textbf{Scope collapse.} The agent edits 30--60\% of th
         +e ground-truth files, then declares the change complete. This i
         +s a process failure, not a reasoning failure: a process-side ha
         +rness that compares the agent's edit set against a discovered c
         +all graph would catch it.                                      
      42 +  \item \textbf{No test artifact.} Across 485 non-PASS runs, 71
         +\% never write or update a test, even when the ground-truth PR 
         +adds tests. A validation-side harness that requires \emph{some}
         + test edit before merge would force the agent to produce a chec
         +kable artifact.                                                
      43 +  \item \textbf{Inconsistent cross-file edits.} Multi-file feat
         +ures (Kubernetes feature-gate, MindSpeed parallelism, CANN oper
         +ator registration) require coordinated edits in 5--15 files; to
         +day's harnesses mostly let the agent stop at the first plausibl
         +e-looking patch. A pre-commit consistency check (signature pari
         +ty, registry membership) would expose these.                   
      44  \end{itemize}
      45  
      46 -The data supports input-side harnessing directly. The following
         - two                                                           
      47 -levels are roadmap items motivated by observed failures rather 
         -than                                                           
      48 -measured ablations:                                            
      49 -\label{sec:harness-roadmap}                                    
      50 -(1)~\emph{language-aware validation}, adapting output checks to
         - Go/Python/C++ conventions;                                    
      51 -(2)~\emph{fix-cycle loops}, feeding validation errors back for 
         -agent correction; and                                          
      52 -(3)~\emph{architecture-aware planning}, enforcing discovery-bef
         -ore-implementation phases and explicit test plans.             
      46 +Each of these failure modes is a harness gap, not a foundation-
         +model deficiency: a sufficiently capable model can already prod
         +uce the right edit when shown the right context.               
      47  
      54 -\finding{Input-side prompt engineering lifts Claude and Codex f
         -rom 0\% to 4.8\% PASS and Cursor/OpenCode from 0\% to 1.6\%. Cl
         -osing the remaining >95\% gap requires output-side and process-
         -side harnesses---fix-cycle loops and enforced test coverage---t
         -hat no current agent implements natively.}                     
      48 +%% ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
         +‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ                                                         
      49 +\subsection{Concrete Recommendations}                          
      50 +\label{sec:harness-recommendations}                            
      51  
      56 -\paragraph{Deployment recipe for near-term production use.}    
      57 -For practitioners shipping coding agents to internal developers
         - today, our data suggests four low-cost hardening steps.       
      58 -(i) \emph{Require a long-form task spec} before agent invocatio
         -n; the $+$2--6~pp PASS uplift we measure for long prompts is th
         -e single largest free-lunch effect in the study.               
      59 -(ii) \emph{Gate outputs on syntax + unit tests} in a short post
         --agent loop; coverage gaps are the clearest harness-addressable
         - failure bucket, so this is cheap.                             
      60 -(iii) \emph{Declare scope explicitly}---list the directories th
         -e agent may touch---to constrain the edit set and avoid the com
         -mon failure of stopping before all required files are identifie
         -d.                                                             
      61 -(iv) \emph{Run an evaluator panel} (minimum two evaluator model
         -s) before merging; we observed 42\%--75\% pairwise agreement be
         -tween judges, so single-judge gating is noisy.                 
      52 +For practitioners deploying coding agents in industrial codebas
         +es today, our data supports four low-cost interventions that ac
         +t as a partial harness:                                        
      53 +                                                               
      54 +\begin{enumerate}                                              
      55 +  \item \textbf{Mandate a structured task brief}, not a chat me
         +ssage. Long prompts lift PASS from 0\% to 4.8\% on the stronges
         +t harnesses with no model change. The cost is one templated for
         +m per task.                                                    
      56 +  \item \textbf{Gate merges on tests + a static scope check.} C
         +overage failures and unmodified-tests-pass should each block, s
         +ince 71\% of our non-PASS runs would have been caught by an aut
         +omatic ``did the agent touch the files the change request impli
         +es'' check.                                                    
      57 +  \item \textbf{Use an evaluator ensemble before human review.}
         + Pairwise judge agreement ranges from 42\% (Codex--GLM) to 74\%
         + (Claude--Cursor); single-judge gating is too noisy, but a 3-of
         +-4 majority resolves 92\% of disputes.                         
      58 +  \item \textbf{Restrict scope to a single subsystem.} Across t
         +he 8 PASS we observe, none touches more than three Kubernetes /
         + Huawei subsystems; multi-subsystem feature work should be spli
         +t before being routed to an agent.                             
      59 +\end{enumerate}                                                
      60 +                                                               
      61 +\paragraph{Limitation.}                                        
      62 +Our results characterise four \emph{deployed} agent harnesses (
         +Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, OpenCode). We did not run contr
         +olled ablations of individual harness components (e.g., the sam
         +e model with vs.\ without scope-check); that experiment is the 
         +natural follow-up.

      1  Frontier coding agents now solve over 70\% of {SWE-bench} bug-fi
         x tasks, prompting industry teams to ask whether they are ready 
         to deliver \emph{feature pull requests}---multi-file, API-bearin
         g, test-and-doc-bundled changes that engineers actually merge.
      2 -We report an industrial-scale assessment that addresses this dep
        -loyment question for Huawei's coding-agent rollout, evaluating f
        -our commercial agents (Claude Opus~4.6, GPT-5.4 / Codex, Compose
        -r-2 / Cursor, GLM-5.1 / OpenCode) on 62 feature-implementation t
        -asks: 12 internal Huawei tasks (CANN operator kernels, MindSpeed
        - training, torch\_npu, and a Kubernetes workflow) and 50 open-so
        -urce tasks from CapBench (Kafka, CPython, Airflow, TiDB, Elastic
        -search, and others).                                            
      2 +We frame each commercial agent as a \emph{harness}---a vendor-bu
        +ilt wrapping of a frontier model with planning, retrieval, tool 
        +calls, and edit policies---and audit four of the leading harness
        +es today: Claude Code (Opus~4.6), Codex CLI (GPT-5.4), Cursor Ag
        +ent (Composer-2), and OpenCode (GLM-5.1). The benchmark covers 6
        +2 feature-implementation tasks: 12 internal Huawei tasks (CANN, 
        +MindSpeed, torch\_npu, Kubernetes) and 50 open-source tasks from
        + the CapBench suite (Kafka, CPython, Airflow, TiDB, Elasticsearc
        +h, and others).                                                 
      3  Each agent is run with both short ($\sim$3-line) and long ($\sim
         $30-line) requirement prompts, producing $4\times62\times2=496$ 
         runs.
      4  Every run is scored independently by four LLM judges (Claude, Co
         dex, Cursor, GLM) on a three-dimension rubric (functional correc
         tness, completeness, behavioral equivalence) with majority-vote 
         (3-of-4) adjudication.
      5
