  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 1 line, removed 3 lines
       9  A long requirement prompt raises Claude/Codex from 0\% to 4.8\%
           PASS but does not rescue weaker agents.
      10  On the engineering side, the dominant non-PASS failure mode is 
          \emph{incomplete scope coverage}: agents understand the require
          ment but stop before touching all the files a real PR has to ch
          ange.
      11  
      12 -These results reframe the open question.                       
      13 -The interesting industrial design space is not "which model wri
         -tes the best diff", but which \emph{harness} (input scaffolding
         -, output validation, scope enforcement, multi-evaluator gating)
         - lets a moderately good model ship feature-level pull requests 
         -safely.                                                        
      14 -We turn this into a four-step deployment recipe and an empirica
         -l lower bound: with 4 leading agents and 496 attempts, current 
         -autonomous coding agents are not yet ready for unsupervised fea
         -ture work in industrial repositories, but their failure modes a
         -re concrete enough to engineer around.                         
      12 +Because the four agents share frontier-class backbone models bu
         +t differ in scaffolding, the 4$\times$ spread between the best 
         +and worst PASS rate is fundamentally a \emph{harness-engineerin
         +g} effect, not a model-capability effect. The interesting indus
         +trial design space is therefore not "which foundation model wri
         +tes the best diff" but which harness elements---input scaffoldi
         +ng, output validation, scope enforcement, multi-evaluator gatin
         +g---let a moderately strong agent ship feature pull requests re
         +liably. We end with a four-step harness-engineering recipe (str
         +uctured prompts, scope contracts, test-coverage gates, evaluato
         +r panels) that reframes our negative result as an actionable de
         +ployment lever: today's agents are not ready to ship features a
         +utonomously, but their failure modes are concrete enough to eng
         +ineer around.

‚Źļ Update(sections/06-harness.tex)
  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 1 line, removed 1 line
      28  \subsection{Input-side Specification Harness: The Prompt-Length
           Effect}
      29  \label{sec:harness-prompt}
      30  
      31 -Within a single agent, the cleanest harness experiment we can r
         -un is the prompt-length manipulation reported in Table~\ref{tab
         -:prompt-effect}. The long prompt is itself an \emph{input-side 
         -harness}: instead of changing the model or runtime, we change t
         -he contract the user signs with the agent before the run starts
         -.                                                              
      31 +Within a single agent, the cleanest harness experiment we can r
         +un is the prompt-length manipulation reported in Table~\ref{tab
         +:prompt}. The long prompt is itself an \emph{input-side harness
         +}: instead of changing the model or runtime, we change the cont
         +ract the user signs with the agent before the run starts.      
      32  
      33  For Claude Code and Codex CLI the input-side harness is decisiv
          e: PASS rate climbs from 0.0\% (short) to 4.8\% (long). For Cur
          sor it is essentially neutral; for OpenCode it lifts the floor 
          from 0.0\% to 1.6\%. In other words, longer specifications only
           help when the agent already has the output- and process-side m
          achinery to act on the extra information; otherwise the extra c
          ontext is wasted.

‚Źļ Update(sections/06-harness.tex)
  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 1 line, removed 1 line
      3  
      4  A frontier LLM does not run a feature pull request alone. In pro
         duction, every coding agent is the composition of \emph{(model, 
         harness)}: the harness wraps the model with file-system tools, a
         n execution loop, retrieval, and an acceptance policy. Section~\
         ref{sec:results} reported results agent-by-agent (Claude Code, C
         odex CLI, Cursor Agent, OpenCode), but read another way the four
          configurations are also four \emph{harness designs}---built by 
         four different vendors---running on top of frontier models that 
         are individually capable on bug-fix benchmarks. This section ref
         rames the gap we measured as a harness comparison: holding the t
         ask constant, how much do harness choices move the needle on ind
         ustrial feature implementation?
      5  
      6 -We use ``harness'' in the same sense as Xia et al.\ (Agentless)~
        -\cite{agentless,xia2024agentless}: the deterministic scaffolding
        - around an LLM that determines what files it sees, when it stops
        -, and how its output is validated. In our setup we cannot fully 
        -isolate the harness from the underlying model, so the comparison
        - is necessarily a \emph{product} comparison rather than an ablat
        -ion. We make the framing explicit so future work can intervene a
        -t the right layer.                                              
      6 +We use ``harness'' in the same sense as Xia et al.\ (Agentless)~
        +\cite{agentless}: the deterministic scaffolding around an LLM th
        +at determines what files it sees, when it stops, and how its out
        +put is validated. In our setup we cannot fully isolate the harne
        +ss from the underlying model, so the comparison is necessarily a
        + \emph{product} comparison rather than an ablation. We make the 
        +framing explicit so future work can intervene at the right layer
        +.

  \title{Are Coding Agents Ready for Feature Pull Requests?\\                   
  An Industrial Audit and Harness-Engineering Playbook}                         
                                                                                
  śąĎśõīśé®ŤćźŤŅôšł™ś†áťĘėÔľĆŤÄĆšłćśėĮŚéüśĚ•ÁöĄÔľö                                              
                                                                                
  From Bug Fixes to Feature Pull Requests:                                      
  An Industrial Audit of Coding Agents and a Case for Harness Engineering       
                                                                                
  ŚéüŚõ†śėĮÔľö

  fail‚ÄĚÔľõśĖįťĘėśöóÁ§ļšĹ†šĽ¨šłćšĽÖŚŹĎÁéįŚ§ĪŤī•ÔľĆŤŅėÁĽôŚáļťÉ®ÁĹ≤Ś∑•Á®čŤ∑ĮŚĺĄ„Äā                         
                                                                                
  ŚŹĮťÄČÁöĄśõīŚ≠¶śúĮÁČąśú¨Ôľö                                                            
                                                                                
  \title{Beyond Bug Fixes: Measuring and Engineering Coding Agents\\            
  for Mergeable Feature Pull Requests}                                          
                                                                                
  ŚŹĮťÄČÁöĄśõīŚ∑•šłöÁČąśú¨Ôľö                                                            
                                                                                
  \title{From SWE-bench Success to Mergeable PRs:\\                             
  What It Takes to Deploy Coding Agents for Feature Work}                       
                                                                                
  ‚łĽ                                                                             
                                                                                
  śĒĻŚÜôŚźéÁöĄśĎėŤ¶Ā                                                                  
                                                                                
  \begin{abstract}                                                              
  Industrial teams are rapidly adopting coding agents, yet most public evidence 
   still comes from bug-fix benchmarks. This leaves a deployment-critical       
  question unanswered: can coding agents produce mergeable \emph{feature pull   
  requests}---multi-file changes that introduce or modify APIs, update tests    
  and documentation, and preserve existing behavior?                            
  We present an industrial audit of four commercial coding-agent harnesses:     
  Claude Code (Opus~4.6), Codex CLI (GPT-5.4), Cursor Agent (Composer-2), and   
  OpenCode (GLM-5.1). We use \emph{harness} to denote the system around a       
  frontier model, including planning, retrieval, tool use, edit policy,         
  validation, and review gates. Our benchmark contains 62                       
  feature-implementation tasks from two complementary settings: 12 internal     
  Huawei tasks from CANN, MindSpeed, torch\_npu, and Kubernetes, and 50         
  open-source tasks from CapBench, covering Kafka, CPython, Airflow, TiDB,      
  Elasticsearch, and other production repositories. Each task is run with both  
  concise and detailed requirements, yielding $4 \times 62 \times 2 = 496$      
  agent executions. Each generated diff is evaluated by a four-agent judging    
  panel using a rubric for functional correctness, scope completeness, and      
  behavioral equivalence, with majority-vote adjudication.                      
  The results reveal a large and actionable \emph{feature-PR gap}. Only 8 of    
  496 executions receive a production-equivalent verdict. The strongest         
  harnesses, Claude Code and Codex CLI, pass 2.4\% of runs; Cursor Agent and    
  OpenCode pass below 1\%. Success is highly concentrated: all passing          
  executions come from only four tasks, while the Kubernetes task family        
  receives no passing verdict across 32 runs. Longer requirements improve       
  Claude Code and Codex CLI from 0\% to 4.8\%, but do not close the gap. The    
  dominant non-pass failure mode is incomplete scope coverage: agents often     
  infer the intended change but stop before modifying all files required for a  
  mergeable PR.                                                                 
  Our main industrial lesson is that feature delivery is a systems problem, not 
   merely a model-selection problem. Differences across harnesses and prompt    
  conditions expose concrete leverage points in input scaffolding, scope        
  contracts, validation gates, and multi-evaluator review. We distill these     
  findings into a harness-engineering playbook for deploying coding agents      
  safely: structured requirements, explicit file/API/test/doc scope contracts,  
  coverage-oriented test gates, and independent evaluator panels. Today's       
  coding agents should not yet autonomously ship feature PRs, but their failure 
   modes are concrete, measurable, and engineerable.                            
  \end{abstract}

  Differences across harnesses and prompt conditions expose concrete leverage   
  points‚Ä¶                                                                       
                                                                                
  ŤŅôś†∑šĽćÁĄ∂šŅĚÁēô‚Äúharness matters‚ÄĚÁöĄŤī°ÁĆģÔľĆšĹÜťĀŅŚÖćšłćŚŹĮŤĮĀÁöĄŚõ†śěúśĖ≠Ť®Ä„ÄāBest-paper       
  ť£éś†ľšłćśėĮśõīśŅÄŤŅõÔľĆŤÄĆśėĮ claim exactly what the data can support„Äā                
                                                                                
  5. śääÁĽďŚįĺšĽé‚Äúnegative result‚ÄĚśĒĻśąź‚Äúactionable deployment lever‚ÄĚ                 
                                                                                
  ŚéüśĎėŤ¶ĀśúÄŚźéŚ∑≤ÁĽŹśúČŤŅôšł™śĖĻŚźĎÔľĆšĹÜśąĎšľöśõīśėéÁ°ģŚúįÁĽĄÁĽáśąźÔľö                              
                                                                                
  Today‚Äôs coding agents should not yet autonomously ship feature PRs, but their 
   failure modes are concrete, measurable, and engineerable.                    
                                                                                
  ŤŅôŚŹ•ŤĮĚťÄāŚźąŚĀöŤģļśĖáÁöĄ take-away„ÄāŚģÉśúČšłČšł™šľėÁāĻÔľö

‚Źļ Update(main.tex)
  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 2 lines, removed 1 line
      14  
      15  \begin{document}
      16  
      17 -\title{From Bug Fixes to Feature Pull Requests:\\An Industrial 
         -Audit of Coding Agents and a Case for Harness Engineering}     
      17 +\title{Are Coding Agents Ready for Feature Pull Requests?\\    
      18 +An Industrial Audit and Harness-Engineering Playbook}          
      19  
      20  \author{Zihan Wu\textsuperscript{$*$}}
      21  \affiliation{\institution{Department of Electrical Engineering,
           City University of Hong Kong}

‚Źļ ÁéįŚú®śĒĻśĎėŤ¶ĀÔľĆśĆČÁĒ®śą∑ŚĽļŤģģÔľö

‚Źļ Write(sections/abstract.tex)
  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 11 lines, removed 12 lines
       1 -Frontier coding agents now solve over 70\% of {SWE-bench} bug-f
         -ix tasks, prompting industry teams to ask whether they are read
         -y to deliver \emph{feature pull requests}---multi-file, API-bea
         -ring, test-and-doc-bundled changes that engineers actually merg
         -e.                                                             
       2 -We frame each commercial agent as a \emph{harness}---a vendor-b
         -uilt wrapping of a frontier model with planning, retrieval, too
         -l calls, and edit policies---and audit four of the leading harn
         -esses today: Claude Code (Opus~4.6), Codex CLI (GPT-5.4), Curso
         -r Agent (Composer-2), and OpenCode (GLM-5.1). The benchmark cov
         -ers 62 feature-implementation tasks: 12 internal Huawei tasks (
         -CANN, MindSpeed, torch\_npu, Kubernetes) and 50 open-source tas
         -ks from the CapBench suite (Kafka, CPython, Airflow, TiDB, Elas
         -ticsearch, and others).                                        
       3 -Each agent is run with both short ($\sim$3-line) and long ($\si
         -m$30-line) requirement prompts, producing $4\times62\times2=496
         -$ runs.                                                        
       4 -Every run is scored independently by four LLM judges (Claude, C
         -odex, Cursor, GLM) on a three-dimension rubric (functional corr
         -ectness, completeness, behavioral equivalence) with majority-vo
         -te (3-of-4) adjudication.                                      
       5 -                                                               
       6 -The benchmark exposes a sharp \emph{feature-implementation gap}
         -.                                                              
       7 -Only 8 of 496 runs reach a production-equivalent verdict (1.6\%
         -); the strongest agents---Claude and Codex---each pass 2.4\% of
         - their runs, Cursor and OpenCode pass under 1\%, and the per-pa
         -ir evaluator agreement is 42--75\%.                            
       8 -The gap is concentrated, not noisy: every PASS comes from one o
         -f just four tasks (C1, C4, M1, T10), and the Kubernetes family 
         -yields 0\% PASS across 32 runs.                                
       9 -A long requirement prompt raises Claude/Codex from 0\% to 4.8\%
         - PASS but does not rescue weaker agents.                       
      10 -On the engineering side, the dominant non-PASS failure mode is 
         -\emph{incomplete scope coverage}: agents understand the require
         -ment but stop before touching all the files a real PR has to ch
         -ange.                                                          
      11 -                                                               
      12 -Because the four agents share frontier-class backbone models bu
         -t differ in scaffolding, the 4$\times$ spread between the best 
         -and worst PASS rate is fundamentally a \emph{harness-engineerin
         -g} effect, not a model-capability effect. The interesting indus
         -trial design space is therefore not "which foundation model wri
         -tes the best diff" but which harness elements---input scaffoldi
         -ng, output validation, scope enforcement, multi-evaluator gatin
         -g---let a moderately strong agent ship feature pull requests re
         -liably. We end with a four-step harness-engineering recipe (str
         -uctured prompts, scope contracts, test-coverage gates, evaluato
         -r panels) that reframes our negative result as an actionable de
         -ployment lever: today's agents are not ready to ship features a
         -utonomously, but their failure modes are concrete enough to eng
         -ineer around.                                                  
       1 +Industrial teams now ask a deployment question, not a benchmark
         + question: \emph{can current coding agents produce feature pull
         + requests}---multi-file, API-changing, test-bearing changes---\
         +emph{that engineers would actually merge}? We answer this for H
         +uawei's coding-agent rollout by auditing four commercial agents
         + on 62 real feature-implementation tasks and reporting where th
         +ey break.                                                      
       2 +We treat each agent as a \emph{harness} on top of a frontier mo
         +del: Claude Code (Opus~4.6), Codex CLI (GPT-5.4), Cursor (Compo
         +ser-2), and OpenCode (GLM-5.1).                                
       3 +We use \emph{harness} to mean the system around the model---pla
         +nning, retrieval, tool use, edit policy, validation hooks, and 
         +review gates.                                                  
       4 +The benchmark covers 62 tasks (12 from Huawei CANN, MindSpeed, 
         +torch\_npu, and Kubernetes workflows; 50 from our open-source e
         +xtension {CapBench}, spanning Kafka, CPython, Airflow, TensorFl
         +ow, Django, and others) and a controlled long/short prompt comp
         +arison, yielding $4\!\times\!62\!\times\!2=496$ runs.          
       5 +Each run is scored by a four-agent LLM panel using a three-dime
         +nsion production-equivalence rubric (functional correctness, co
         +mpleteness, behavioral equivalence) and a strict majority verdi
         +ct.                                                            
       6 +Across 496 runs, only 8 reach a production-equivalent verdict (
         +1.6\%); the strongest harnesses (Claude Code, Codex CLI) pass 2
         +.4\% each, the weaker harnesses (Cursor, OpenCode) pass under 1
         +\%.                                                            
       7 +Every PASS run concentrates on four of the 62 tasks, and the en
         +tire Kubernetes track yields 0/32 PASS.                        
       8 +The dominant failure mode is harness-side, not model-side: agen
         +ts typically infer the correct goal from the task description b
         +ut cover only 30--60\% of the ground-truth file set before stop
         +ping, omit regression tests and generated artifacts, and skip c
         +ross-module wiring.                                            
       9 +Long prompts lift the strongest harnesses from 0\% to 4.8\% PAS
         +S while leaving Cursor and OpenCode flat, suggesting that gains
         + from richer specification are mediated by the harness, not the
         + underlying model.                                             
      10 +We close with a four-step harness recipe (long-form task spec, 
         +scope contracts, output-side test/coverage gating, multi-evalua
         +tor acceptance) that follows directly from where today's agents
         + fail in our audit.                                            
      11 +The takeaway for industry teams is concrete: ship coding agents
         + now for low-coupling tasks, but invest harness engineering---n
         +ot just larger models---to close the feature-PR gap.

  śėĮŚŹĮŚĀöÁöĄ„ÄāÁĽôšłÄšł™Ś∑•Á®čŚĆĖśĖĻś°ą,Áõģś†áśėĮ reviewer ÁúčŚąį"śąĎšĽ¨ LLM
  Śą§ŚģėśúČšļļŚ∑•ťĒöŚģö"ŚįĪŤÉĹśĒĺŤŅáŤŅôšłÄŚÖ≥„Äā

  ŚĽļŤģģśĖĻś°ą: Human verification on the 96-run Huawei subset

  1) ŤĆÉŚõī & Ś∑•šĹúťáŹ

  - ŚÖ®ťáŹ 96 runs (12 tasks √ó 4 agents √ó 2 prompts) ‚ÄĒ šłćśäĹś†∑,Śõ†šłļŚŹ™śúČŤŅôšłÄś°£śėĮ
  industry ÁöĄ ground truth
  - śĮŹśĚ° ~10‚Äď15 min(ŤĮĽ prompt + ŤĮĽ GT diff + ŤĮĽ agent diff + šłČÁĽīŚļ¶śČďŚąÜ)
  - śÄĽŚ∑•śó∂ ~16‚Äď20 hours,1 šļļŚŹĮŚú® 2‚Äď3 šł™Ś∑•šĹúśó•ŚģĆśąź

  ŚģěśĖĹś≠•ť™§

  Step 1 ‚ÄĒ ŚáÜŚ§á rating sheet (Ś∑≤śúČ LLM ŤĮĄŚąÜ,ŚŹ™ťúÄšļļŚ∑• override)
  ŚĮľŚáļ 96 runs ŚĹďŚČćÁöĄ 4 šł™ LLM judge ÁöĄ A/B/C ŚąÜśēį + verdict Śąį CSV,Śä† 4
  šł™Á©ļŚąó:human_A, human_B, human_C, human_verdict, notes„Äā

  Step 2 ‚ÄĒ ŤĮĄŚąÜŚćŹŤģģ (šłÄŤáīśÄßŚÖ≥ťĒģ)
  - śĮŹśĚ°:śČďŚľÄ experiment_dir/„ÄĀgt_diff.patch„ÄĀprompt.md,śĆČÁéįśúČ rubric ÁĽô 0‚Äď5 ŚąÜ
  - ŤĮĄŚąÜŚČć šłćŤ¶Ā Áúč LLM ÁöĄŚąÜśēį(ťĀŅŚÖć anchoring)
  - ŤĮĄŚģĆŚźé reveal LLM ŚąÜśēį,Śú® notes ťáĆŤģįŚĹē disagreement ÁĪĽŚěč(under-counted scope
   / over-counted style / Á≠Č)

  Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Ťģ°ÁģóŚÖ≥ťĒģśĆáś†á
  - agreement rate = #(human_verdict == LLM_majority) / 96
  - őļ (Cohen / Fleiss őļ) human vs each LLM judge,šĽ•ŚŹä LLM-majority
  - bias direction:LLM ŚÄĺŚźĎšļé PASS-leaning ŤŅėśėĮ FAIL-leaning(ŚąÜŚąęÁĽüŤģ° LLM=PASS
  but human=FAIL,ŚŹćšĻčšļ¶ÁĄ∂)

  Step 4 ‚ÄĒ ŚÜôŤŅõ paper(Śú® ¬ß3 evaluation methodology śąĖ ¬ß8 threats)

  ÁĽô reviewer ÁúčÁöĄŚÖ≥ťĒģśēįŚ≠óś®°śĚŅ

  ‚Ėé "On the 96 Huawei runs (12 tasks √ó 4 agents √ó 2 prompts) we performed an 
  ‚Ėé independent human review by [N] engineers familiar with the Huawei 
  ‚Ėé codebases. Human/LLM-panel verdict agreement was X%; Cohen's őļ = Y. Of 8

      129 +# Binary PASS vs not-PASS                                     
      130 +def b(v): return 1 if v == "PASS" else 0                      
      131 +hb = [b(human[k]) for k in human if k in llm]                 
      132 +lb = [b(llm[k]) for k in human if k in llm]                   
      133 +n = len(hb)                                                   
      134 +agree_b = sum(1 for x,y in zip(hb,lb) if x==y)                
      135 +ph = sum(hb)/n; pl = sum(lb)/n                                
      136 +po_b = agree_b/n                                              
      137 +pe_b = ph*pl + (1-ph)*(1-pl)

      203 +\textbf{Human pilot calibration.}                             
      204 +To check that the LLM panel is not idiosyncratically biased re
          +lative to                                                     
      205 +domain-expert review, we conducted a blind human-rater pilot o
          +n all 96

      19  Agent capabilities evolve rapidly: the model snapshots we use (
          Claude Opus~4.6, GPT-5.4, Composer-2, GLM-5.1) will be supersed
          ed within months of publication.
      20  We expect relative patterns---the Kubernetes cliff, long-prompt
           lift, and mixed semantic/coverage failure profile---to persist
          , but absolute PASS rates are a lower bound under today's model
          s.
      21  
      22 -\textbf{Evaluator honesty.}                                    
      22 +\textbf{LLM-vs-human-judge calibration.}                       
      23 +We validated the LLM panel against human ratings on the 96 Huaw
         +ei cells (12 tasks $\times$ 4 agents $\times$ 2 prompts).  Thre
         +e-way verdict agreement was 66.7\% (Cohen's $\kappa = 0.38$, fa
         +ir-to-moderate), and PASS-vs-non-PASS agreement was 92.7\% ($\k
         +appa = 0.47$).  The LLM panel was more conservative than humans
         +: 9 cells were rated \textsc{pass} by humans vs.\ 4 by the stri
         +ct-majority panel, and every panel-\textsc{pass} was a human-\t
         +extsc{pass}.  No human-\textsc{fail} was overturned to \textsc{
         +pass} by the panel.  The headline 1.6\% PASS rate is therefore 
         +an under-, not over-estimate of merge-readiness, and the deploy
         +ment story (very few feature-complete agent PRs) holds under ei
         +ther rubric.                                                   
      24 +                                                               
      25 +\textbf{Evaluator-honesty risk.}                               
      26  A persistent risk with LLM-as-judge evaluation is inflated or d
          eflated scoring from the judge's own biases.
      27  We mitigate this three ways: (i) four independent judges from d
          istinct model families (ii) explicit rubric anchors at each of 
          six score levels (iii) a structured JSON output that exposes pe
          r-dimension scores for audit.
      28  Empirically, judges disagree most on \emph{partial} verdicts (s
          pread of 1.2 on the 0--5 scale), and least on \emph{PASS} verdi
          cts where file-level coverage is decisive.
